This approach: - Assumes something is broken when understanding is the real goal - Provides no learning opportunity - Results in copy-pasting without comprehension - Misses the chance to explore underlying concepts ✅ Good Prompt: "Explain this Python function line by line. What does the yield keyword do here? If I changed while count < n to while a < n, what would happen?" This approach led to: Claude walking me through each line, explaining tuple unpacking, loop logic, and the critical distinction between `return` and `yield`. I learned that `yield` turns a regular function into a generator—essentially a "pause button" that makes the function memory-efficient by generating values on demand rather than creating all results at once. When I asked about changing the loop condition, [...] code. Rather than surface-level responses, Claude excels at breaking down complex concepts line-by-line, explaining not just what the code does, but why it works that way. This depth is crucial for building genuine understanding rather than just copying solutions. One of the most powerful aspects of learning with Claude is the ability to explore hypothetical scenarios. Questions like "What would happen if I changed X to Y?" get detailed, accurate responses that help build intuition about how code behaves. Good vs. Bad Prompts To illustrate the difference between effective and ineffective AI interaction, I tested both approaches with a Python Fibonacci generator function that uses the `yield` keyword. ❌ Bad Prompt: "Fix this code." results.title: Recent reasoning research: GRPO tweaks, base model RL, and ...
results.content:
GRPO p4. reward shaping for avoiding truncation

This is the most minor change in DAPO. Essentially, they add a soft and then a firm penalty as the model goes over in generation length. For a model with a max context length of 16k tokens, they start applying a penalty at 12k tokens and linearly increase it to 16k. This length control mechanism feels like it’ll be outdated, or will be a really minor trick in the future.

For reference, many RL implementations already include some larger penalty if the model truncates itself (i.e. never generates an EOS token or answer).

Image [...] Moving the length normalization 1/|o| outside the group sum makes the loss calculated just by summing over the tokens on the inside of both sums.

Image

Here, the groups of answers are normalized by the token counts for that prompt overall. Default GRPO normalizes the loss per token for only the response that the token corresponds to.

This is cool but needs more replication! See discussion below. In their setup, they see quite different behavior.

The idea is to enable learning better from long answers. Good long answers should be adequately rewarded and repetitive, bad, long answers need to be penalized.

GRPO p4. reward shaping for avoiding truncation [...] Relation to GRPO training failures

Some failures we’ve seen in our early research, which I’ve heard from other laboratories too, are that GRPO can fail by starting to generate very repetitive and long responses. The sequence length of the answers blows up to the maximum allowed in the training setup and the performance on downstream evaluations drops to 0. These potential interventions, for Dr. GRPO and DAPO both could help alleviate this. We’ll have independent replications soon!

Relation to existing RLHF infrastructure

results.content: 3.3 GRPO Fails to Improve Pass@N Figure 2 presents model performance on Dval, eval-uated up to pass@512. GRPO substantially boosts 25550 1 2 4 8 16 32 64 128 256 512 N 0.50 0.55 0.60 0.65 0.70 0.75 0.80 0.85 Pass@N Model DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5-SFT GRPO-Default Figure 2: Finetuning DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5-SFT with GRPO, evaluated on Dval. GRPO improves pass@N significantly for small N, but performs worse than the base model for large N. We aim to understand this behavior and develop methods to overcome it. This sharpens the distribu-tion and impairs pass@N performance for large N. Our unlikeliness reward addresses rank bias by explicitly encouraging uplifting low-probability correct
